title: Three India-Flagged Crude Tankers Clear Hormuz With 94 Crew, First Transit Since Ceasefire
summary: Three India-flagged crude tankers — Desh Vaibhav, Desh Vibhor, and Sanmar Herald — have successfully transited the Strait of Hormuz with 94 crew aboard, the first such passage since the ceasefire took effect. The ships carry 8.6 lakh metric tonnes of crude and are due in Indian ports between June 24 and July 1, a tangible signal that energy supply chains are beginning to normalise after months of disruption.

coverages.headline: Three India-flagged oil tankers cross Hormuz with 94 crew onboard
coverages.excerpt: The first transit of Indian-flagged vessels since the ceasefire took effect; ships carry 8.6 lakh MT of crude and are due in Indian ports between June 24 and July 1 — a significant signal that energy supply chains are normalising.
coverages.bias_notes: Naming all three vessels and the precise crew count (94) gives this story a level of verifiable specificity that's valuable after months of more abstract reporting on blockade impacts. 'Significant signal that energy supply chains are normalising' is a reasonable read of the facts, but it's worth treating as a single data point rather than a trend — three tankers safely transiting once does not yet establish that the ceasefire will hold reliably for the sustained volume of traffic India's energy security actually requires. The same ceasefire is reported elsewhere today as having already been violated multiple times, which this piece doesn't mention.
